caption:
"We kicked off our first day of our 32nd Escucha Mi Grito Conference with a Mural Tour at the @uiclcc. We got an in depth tour of the many murals and the history of the center. 🖼️

During the event the archives were available to look through. There was binders from the history of the LCC from 1976 to now, information on Rafael Cintron Ortiz who the center is named after, and even an archive of some of the work from MeSA over the years. 

If you’re ever interested in visiting the Latino Cultural Center it is a space to study and come together as community during their programs. You can also look through their many archives in their office 🗂️".

caption:
"As we wrap up the semester, we wanted to highlight some of our biggest programming for the Spring semester- EARTH MONTH! For our first event, we hosted a conversation between two Chicago based artists-  Delilah Salgado @artofzena and Melissa Gutierrez, creator of @nobadfruit.

Delilah and Melissa shared insights on how they merge themes of environmental and food justice within their own art practices. 

For Delilah, she shared how her upbringing in Gage Park and interest in a feminist-forward hip hop scene inspired much of the imagery she stills plays with today. More recently, she spoke on her shift in symbolism and imagery as she continues to fight for clean air in her now neighborhood, McKinley Park which is being impacted by big polluter MAT Asphalt. 

Melissa shared that growing up, she saw the way her family, the local community and farmers worked hard together to grow their food and make sure none of it went to waste. Seeing how the U.S has millions of pounds of food waste every year and yet many around the world face food insecurity, Melissa created NoBadFruit that focuses on improving the world, one bite at a time, by spreading awareness and education on food waste. 

We kept our momentum going with a film screening of the @youngcutulturalstewards documentary, “We Are Each Other’s Harvest.” After watching the film, we were joined by a panel  of Stephan Hamer (@growgreaterenglewood), Dulce Morales (@cedillofreshproduce), Viviana Moreno (La Villita Farm @lvejo20) and filmmaker Ally Almore. In the discussion, they talked about their own journeys in becoming land stewards and the challenges that arose as being BIPOC and/or LGBTQIA+. With increased cuts to funding, the panelists also touched on the importance of their organizations and how to sustain the work despite decreases in funding and resources. 

Make sure to check out and support Grow Greater Englewood, Cedillo Fresh Produce, and La Villita Farm to uplift our local land stewards! 

Thank you to @sustainableuic for funding and @uiclibrary for co-sponsoring our Earth Month program series! Make sure to join us next week for LCC Snack bags and free coffee during our regular hours 💙".

caption:
"Last week, we hosted our Spring semester's Noche de Poetas! 

This is the LCC's longest running public program - happening since the '90s! In true open mic night fashion, we have continued our tradition of the "community poem." 

Throughout the night, a notebook is passed around and each person is encouraged to add one line to the poem based on that night's theme. Afterwards, the poem is read aloud by the student organizations facilitating the open mic. 

Read what students and audience members wrote on the theme of "ANOTHER WORLD IS HAPPENING!" 🐛🦋🌎🌈🐝".

caption:
"Celebrate Earth Month with the UIC Latino Cultural Center! 🐛🦋

This year’s theme, “Another World is Happening,” aims to highlight the critical resistance and ground work being done by local communities amidst a dire climate crisis. Through an environmental justice (EJ) perspective, this program series serves as a way to envision and act toward a more just environmental future. We will highlight the current EJ work and organizing currently being done by our local Chicago communities. 🌈

When: Wednesday, April 23rd
Time: 2pm-4pm 
Where: UIC Latino Cultural Center-Lecture Center B2

🎉 Festival is FREE and OPEN TO ALL AUDIENCES! We hope you join us! 🎉

***Festival will take place indoors and outdoors, weather permitting. Festival will go on rain or shine. 

LCC Earth Month Arts Festival 2025: ANOTHER WORLD IS HAPPENING! 

Celebrate Earth Month with us and enjoy these free activities open to the public! 

Happening outside the LCC: 
🦋 Screenprinting with @marimachamonarcapress 
☕ Free locally roasted coffee with @anticonquistacafe 
 📸 Photo Booth (Backdrop by @magdadoesart and Props by @artistic_disasters)
📚 UIC Pop-Up Library @uiclibrary 
🌻 Chalk Art
🐛 LCC Scavenger Hunt
🌟 Button-Making

Inside the Gallery: 
Get to know these local Chicago community organizations! 
ceibacolectiva @chicago_chrp @lvejo20 @n4ejchicago @pilsen_perro @pcrchi @thefreshwaterlab @uicheritagegarden @sustainableuic 

❓Where are we located?
The Latino Cultural Center is accessible via public transit including the CTA Blue Line and bus lines including the 60, 8, among others. We are located on East campus right next to the main quad. 

This event is a part of the annual LCC Earth Month programming. This program series is funded by @sustainableuic and co-sponsored by @uiclibrary.".

caption:
"Join us TOMORROW, APRIL 15TH for a Conversation Between Local Artists About Environmental and Food Justice in Chicago ft. @artofzena and @nobadfruit! 

When: April 15th (TOMORROW!!)
Time: 1pm-2pm
Where: UIC Latino Cultural Center-Lecture Center B2

This program is free and open to all audiences! 💫

@artofzena is a first-generation Mexican American public artist, educator, curator, and activist based in McKinley Park, Chicago. Rooted in intersectional feminism, Chicano culture, and Underground Hip Hop, her community-based practice addresses environmental racism while using art as a tool for healing and empowerment.

Salgado currently serves as the artist advisory coordinator for the Pilsen Latina Legacies project under the Chicago Monuments Project, in collaboration with lead artist Diana Solis. She co-created the mosaic and papel picado inspired sculpture Rio de Bienvenida / River of Welcome at the Canalport Riverwalk alongside Cynthia Weiss, Citlalli Trujillo, and Freshwater Labs.

Having grown up in Gage Park and later raising a family in McKinley Park, Salgado draws deep inspiration from her experiences of disinvestment, resilience, and reconnection with nature. Her work uplifts youth and families, advocating for community-driven green space investment and the integration of nature into everyday urban life.

@nobadfruit NoBadFruit is an art brand that focuses on improving the world, one bite at a time, by spreading awareness and education on food waste. Through the approach of art, merchandise, apparel, murals and events, they strive to create a mindful, kinder, and sustainable planet. Together, we can limit the amount of hunger and teach the importance of resources that go into growing food.

This program is a part of our annual Earth Month programming series. See our previous posts for other events happening this month! 

This program is funded by the @sustainableuic and co-sponsored by @uiclibrary.".

caption:
"Last Wednesday, we hosted our second Civic Cinema program of the semester! We screened “En San Isidro” and welcomed visiting LALS Cuban scholar Anamely Ramos Gonzalez for a post-film conversation. 🎥

The film “En San Isidro” by Katherine Bisquet followed a group of artists, cultural workers, friends, family, and other Cubans who camped inside a house in San Isidro, Cuba, in response to the unjust arrest of rapper Denis Solís, in November 2020. Solís was abducted by officers of the Cuban National Revolutionary Police Force. After his trial, he was sentenced to eight months imprisonment under alleged contempt of court. Those who barricaded themselves into the home all engaged in protest in different forms - from hunger strikes to thirst strikes. Cuban police reacted violently and often prevented food, water, and money from entering the home. In the end, the home was raided by police during the COVID-19 pandemic. 

Anamely Ramos is a Latin American and Latino Studies (LALS) Department visiting Cuban scholar. After the film, Ramos had an open discussion about her experiences as an artist in Cuba and the political state of artists and activists in Cuba. Ramos spoke from firsthand experiences as she was one of those who barricaded herself in the home in protest. After the discussion, students had the opportunity to check out books centered around the themes of Cuba and activism towards its government, provided by the @uiclibrary.

Thank you to @uiclibrary and @lalsatuic for co-sponsoring the event, and thank you to the UIC Library for allowing us to access and check out books on Cuba after the event. 📚 Don’t forget to attend our Earth Month programming starting next week! 🌎🌎".

Time: 12pm-2pm

caption:
"Last Wednesday, we hosted our first Civic Cinema of the semester! We screened the short film Jamaica & Tamarindo: Afro Tradition in the Heart of Mexico and enjoyed fresh aguas frescas! The film centered around the lives of five Afro-Mexicanos and how they navigated their identities while growing up through challenges of racism, isolatedness, and unfamiliarity with their intersecting heritages. Through the exploration of jamaica, a popular flower used for culinary, textile, and medicinal use, and tamarind, a leguminous fruit often used in spices, they were able to connect to their African roots with the plants’ prominence and importance in Mexican culture. 🌺

Students were able to enjoy the film and stay for a post-film discussion moderated by Diosmery Roque. Disomery discussed her own journey discovering her Afro-Mexican roots later in life and how that became a crucial part of her identity. Additionally, Diosmery brought her own personal collection of Afro-latinidad books for students to look through. During the Q&A, Disomery discussed how her upbringing and lack of representation of African heritage affected her sense of belonging in her communities.

Students were able to check out the @uiclibrary Pop-Up Library during the event. The library brought a variety of different genres of books ranging from fantasy, personal memoirs, fiction, and historical books connecting to Afro- Latinidad. Students were able to check out books directly at the event! 📚

Thank you so much to Diosmery @dor_doom for facilitating our post-screening conversation. Also thank you to the @bccatuic and @sdsatuic for co-sponsoring this event and to @uiclibrary for bringing their pop-up library to our space!

Make sure to join us for our next Civic Cinema on Wednesday, April 2nd from 1pm-2pm where we will be watching En San Isidro and discussing afterwards! ✨".

caption:
"❗❗❗ Join us for our last virtual Zona Abierta of the semester ft. Dr.Kumar Ramanathan from the Department of Political Science!

❗Reviving Local Democracy: 
New Conflicts in the Chicago City Council 
 
Presented by the UIC Latino Cultural Center 
 
WHEN: Wednesday, April 16, 2025 | 2-3pm CST 

WHERE: Virtually on Zoom 
RSVP: https://go.uic.edu/ZA416

Join us as UIC postdoctoral scholars share their experiences and respective research with the UIC community. Through an intersectional lens, these scholars reveal the complexity and specificity of their contemporary research and how the lives of those in the margins are impacted. 

Dr. Kumar Ramanathan, from the Department of Political Science, will present ongoing research on the politics of the contemporary Chicago City Council. His research shows how conflicts over inequality have transformed the Council—after decades where alderpeople were content to let the mayor control policy making, they are now asserting their role as legislators. He will discuss the new dividing lines within the Council and what they mean for the future of Chicago politics.

For access needs or questions, email lcc@uic.edu or call 312-996-3095. Auto-captions enabled on Zoom. ASL Interpretation services may be available by contacting the LCC 5 business days before the program. All audiences are welcome. 

Make sure to RSVP! Program is free and open to all audiences!".

caption:
"❗️🎞️🎥CIVIC CINEMA - SPRING 2025 🎥 🎞️ ❗️

“Inside San Isidro” by Katherine Bisquet

All audiences are welcome. Run Time: 28 mins. 

When: Wednesday, April 2nd, 2025
Where: Latino Cultural Center-Lecture Center B2
Time: 1pm-2pm

Join us for a short film and conversation surrounding the 2020 San Isidro artist protests in Cuba. In November 2020, rapper Denis Solís was kidnapped by officers of the Cuban National Revolutionary Police Force and sentenced to eight months of imprisonment. In protest, a group of activists barricaded themselves inside the headquarters of the San Isidro organization to demand freedom for Solís and all Cubans. 

Post-screening conversation facilitated by LALS Visiting Cuban Scholar Anamely Ramos González.

Post-film conversation is in Spanish with English subtitles. Conversation will be held primarily in Spanish, with English interpretation available. 

We will once again be joined by the UIC Pop-Up Library! After the program, browse a selection of titles related to the themes of the film courtesy of the UIC Library! UIC students, staff and faculty are able to check-out books during the event. Books will need to be need to be returned to the UIC University library. 📚 

This program is co-sponsored by @lalsatuic and @uiclibrary.

We hope to see you there! 🎉🎥🎞️".

caption:
"Join us for our upcoming 🎥📽️CIVIC CINEMA 🎞️ happening this THURSDAY! 

🌺Jamaica y Tamarindo: Afro Tradition in the Heart of Mexico 🌺 A short film by Ebony Bailey

Presented by the @uiclcc and @bccatuic

In addition to being joined by Chicago cultural worker @dor_doom for a post-screening facilitation and brief presentation on Afro-Mexican identity in Guerrero, we will now be joined by the @uiclibrary's Mobile Pop-Up Library and @sdsatuic! 

📚 The UIC Pop-Up Library will bring a selection of titles related to the themes of the film and UIC students will be able check-out a book during the program! 📚

We hope to see you there! Below is more information on Thursday's program: 

When: Thursday, March 13th, 2025
Where: Latino Cultural Center- Lecture Center B2
Time: 1pm-2pm

“Jamaica y Tamarindo: Afro Tradition in the Heart of Mexico” spotlights the lives of five diverse Afro-Mexicanos as they explore their intersecting identities through their heritages while living in Mexico City. Stay for a brief post-film conversation featuring Diosmery Roque and enjoy fresh aguas frescas!

Film Duration: 20 min. Film is in Spanish with English subtitles. Post-screening conversation will happen in English.

Aguas frescas are first come, first served.

For access needs or questions, email lcc@uic.edu or call 312-996-3095. The LCC is wheelchair accessible. Auto-captions will be available during screening. All audiences are welcome.

This program is co-sponsored by the @uiclibrary & @sdsatuic

Diosmery is a first-generation college graduate with a Bachelor’s degree in Sociology and History from Roosevelt University. She is of Afro-Mexican roots in the state of Guerrero. Where she visits every year in December. Diosmery currently lives in DuPage County, where she continues her journey to decolonize. Her favorite snack is fried Plantains! After the screening, Diosmery will facilitate a conversation about the film and will briefly present on Afro-Mexican identity in Guerrero.
———
The LCC Civic Cinema series shows documentary films to engage different voices on campus and neighboring communities in dialogue about pressing social and environmental issues affecting our lives. 🎞️".

"❗🧠 Zona Abierta: Mental Health Among Communities of Color 🧠❗

Presented by the UIC Latino Cultural Center 

💡Join us as UIC postdoctoral scholars share their experiences and respective research with the UIC community. Through an intersectional lens, these scholars reveal the complexity and specificity of their contemporary research and how the lives of those in the margins are impacted. 

WHEN: Wednesday, March 19, 2025 | 2-3pm CST 

WHERE: Virtually on Zoom 
RSVP: https://go.uic.edu/ZA319

🧠 Dr. Abhery Das, from the Division of Health Policy & Administration in the School of Public Health, will present on the inequities in social systems and mental health among communities of color. She will discuss two studies which focus on how racialized economic segregation impacts the suicide among Black youth and how hate crimes correspond with greater psychiatric emergencies among Asian Americans. 

❗For access needs or questions, email lcc@uic.edu or call 312-996-3095. Auto-captions enabled on Zoom. ASL Interpretation services may be available by contacting the LCC 5 business days before the program. All audiences are welcome.

We hope to see you there! Make sure to RSVP via: go.uic.edu/ZA319
------
The LCC Zona Abierta series consist of public programs that highlight the intersection of arts, humanities, science, culture and civic life with presentations by local, national and international artists, scholars and community leaders about pressing social and environmental issues affecting the lives of Latines and Latin Americans, while making connections with other communities.".

Biological Causes of Neurodevelopmental Issues: The Role of Environmental Exposures...featuring Dr. Victor Florez-Garcia 🎉

Join us as UIC postdoctoral scholars share their experiences and respective research with the UIC community. Through an intersectional lens, these scholars reveal the complexity and specificity of their contemporary research and how the lives of those in the margins are impacted.

WHERE: Virtually on Zoom

Dr. Florez-Garcia, from the Division of Epidemiology & Biostatistics, will present previous research on the impact of metal exposures on children's intellectual performance in Mexico. His research employs cutting-edge epidemiological methods to investigate the role of environmental pollutants in developing multiple chronic conditions.

caption:
"Earlier this week, us alongside the @arabamcc hosted a Tatreez, Bordado, Embriodery event led by Palestinian Tatreez artist @tatreezjenin ! This program was co-developed with @sjpuic and @mesaatuic with additional support from @uicwlrc and @uicgws. 🧵🇵🇸

This was a collaborative public program between the @uiclcc and @arabamcc where we learned the similarities between Tatreez and Bordado (embroidery) in Mexico and Central America! This educational ARTivism program highlights how our very own UIC Centers for Cultural Understanding and Social Change (CCUSC) work across our diverse populations to help us better understand similar and different cultural practices. 🧵🍉

Students were able to learn about the cultural similarities between embroidery in Palestine and Latin America, while taking home their very own Tatreez projects! Stay tuned for our future events.

Lisa Kurian Phillips of @wbezchicago wrote an article featuring the event. You can read the article at: https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/02/20/uic-cultural-centers-and-race-based-college-programs-across-illinois-threatened-by-latest-trump-guidance

Photos 1 & 2 courtesy of Lisa Kurian Phillips.".

caption:
"🇵🇸 🍉 Meet the Artist: Tatreez Jenin! 🇵🇸 🍉 

Get to know the artist for our upcoming Tatreez event, highlighting the connections between Palestine, Mexico and Central America! 

@tatreezjenin is a Palestinian tatreez artist that started Tatreez Jenin in 2024 after recognizing the lack of tatreez resources and education in the diaspora. She is a self-taught tatreezer and is deeply passionate about keeping Palestinian cultures and history alive. For Jenin, a threaded needle and fabric are tools of resistance and empowers members of her community to dive into the deep ocean of Palestinian embroidery. It is how we keep Palestinian voices alive and how she aims to humanize the deep identities of Palestinians. 🧵🪡🇵🇸

This upcoming program kicks off @sjpuic's Apartheid Week. Check out their page for their full spread of events. Viva Palest!na!🇵🇸🇵🇸🇵🇸

This program is a collaboration between the @uiclcc and @arabamcc. Co-developed by @sjpchicago by and @mesaatuic. Co-sponsored @uicwlrc and the @uicgws at UIC.

If you have RSVP'd to this program and you will NO longer be able to attend, email nayala3@uic.edu ASAP to give your spot to someone else! This event is currently at full capacity.".
